timeline.event: Born in Myeongdong-chon, North Gando
Entered Myeongdong Elementary School and published the literary magazine 'New Myeongdong'
Graduated from Myeongdong Elementary School and transferred to Hwaryong County First Elementary School
Transferred to Soongsil Middle School in Pyongyang and published the poem 'Fantasy'
Transferred to Gwangmyeong Middle School after the closure of Soongsil Middle School
Graduated from Gwangmyeong Middle School
Entered Yonhi Technical College
Published a poem in 'Sonyeon' (Boy) magazine and received his first manuscript fee
Changed his name to 'Hiranuma' under the Japanese name-changing policy
Graduated from the Department of Literature at Yonhi Technical College
Entered the Department of English Literature at Rikkyo University in Tokyo
Transferred to the Department of English Literature at Doshisha University in Kyoto
Arrested by the Japanese police on charges of violating the Public Security Preservation Law
Sentenced to 2 years in prison by the Kyoto District Court and imprisoned in Fukuoka Prison
Died in prison at Fukuoka Prison
His posthumous works were first introduced in the Kyunghyang Shinmun and a memorial service was held
A preliminary edition of his posthumous poetry collection 'Sky, Wind, Star and Poem' was provisionally published
His posthumous poetry collection 'Sky, Wind, Star and Poem' was officially published
The 'Yun Dong-ju Monument' engraved with his posthumous work 'Prologue' was erected at Yonsei University
The Yun Dong-ju Literature Award was established and the first recipient was selected
Posthumously awarded the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Independent Medal
The 'Prologue' monument was erected at Yongjeong Middle School
The 'Prologue' monument was erected at Doshisha University in Japan
Selected as a Korean artist who brightened the 20th century by the Korean Association of Art Critics

keyAchievements.title: Activities as an Independence Activist, Poet, and Writer
keyAchievements.description: He was active as a Korean independence activist, poet, and writer during the Japanese colonial period.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of the Literary Magazine 'New Myeongdong'
keyAchievements.description: During his time at Myeongdong Elementary School, he published the literary magazine 'New Myeongdong' to begin his literary activities.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Debut
keyAchievements.description: While attending Yonhi Technical College, he officially debuted in the literary world by publishing a poem in the magazine 'Sonyeon'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Posthumous Poetry Collection 'Sky, Wind, Star and Poem'
keyAchievements.description: After his death, his posthumous poetry collection 'Sky, Wind, Star and Poem', containing most of his works, was published and caused a great sensation.

keyAchievements.title: Recognized as a Conscientious Intellectual
keyAchievements.description: He was recognized as a conscientious intellectual in the late Japanese colonial period, and his poetry contains criticism of Japanese imperialism and self-reflection.

keyAchievements.title: Evaluated as a Nationalist Resistance Poet
keyAchievements.description: He is evaluated as a nationalist resistance poet and a poet with a strong will and gentle lyricism.

keyAchievements.title: Selected as the Most Beloved Poet by Koreans
keyAchievements.description: In 1991, he was selected as the poet most loved by Koreans.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Award of the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Independent Medal
keyAchievements.description: In 1990, the South Korean government posthumously awarded him the Order of Merit for National Foundation, Independent Medal to honor his contributions.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Yun Dong-ju Literature Award
keyAchievements.description: To inherit his poetic spirit, the Yun Dong-ju Literature Award was established by the Korean Writers Association in 1985.

faq.question: When and where was Yun Dong-ju born?
faq.answer: Yun Dong-ju was born on December 30, 1917, in Myeongdong-chon, North Gando.

faq.question: What schools did Yun Dong-ju graduate from?
faq.answer: He graduated from Myeongdong Elementary School and Jilin Gwangmyeong Middle School, and attended Yonhi Technical College.

faq.question: What is Yun Dong-ju's representative poetry collection?
faq.answer: Most of his works are contained in the posthumous poetry collection 'Sky, Wind, Star and Poem' published after his death.

faq.question: Why was Yun Dong-ju arrested in Japan?
faq.answer: On July 14, 1943, he was arrested by the Japanese police as an ideological offender under the Public Security Preservation Law and imprisoned in Fukuoka Prison.

faq.question: When did Yun Dong-ju die?
faq.answer: Yun Dong-ju died in prison at Fukuoka Prison on February 16, 1945.

faq.question: Is it true that Yun Dong-ju changed his name to a Japanese style?
faq.answer: In late 1941, he changed his name to 'Hiranuma', and the prevailing view is that this was an unavoidable choice for studying abroad in Japan.

faq.question: How is Yun Dong-ju evaluated?
faq.answer: He is evaluated as a nationalist resistance poet and a poet with strong will and gentle lyricism, and his poetry contains the agony and self-reflection of a colonial intellectual.

timeline.event: Born in Colonus, an administrative district of Athens, as the son of a wealthy knight.
Selected as a lead choir boy to sing at a banquet celebrating the Battle of Salamis.
At the age of 29, he gained fame by defeating his senior Aeschylus in the tragic drama competition at the City Dionysia.
Elected as one of the 10 commanders of the Delian League.
Entered political life, held several key positions, and served as a naval admiral.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: One of the Three Great Tragic Poets of Greece
keyAchievements.description: Along with Aeschylus and Euripides, he is considered one of the three great tragic poets of ancient Greece.

keyAchievements.title: Victories in Tragic Drama Competitions
keyAchievements.description: He won 1st place 24 times in the tragic drama competitions at the City Dionysia, earning the title of the perfecter of tragic art.

keyAchievements.title: Innovation in Tragic Art
keyAchievements.description: He abolished the trilogy format, increased the number of chorus members, introduced a third actor, and adopted props and background painting, contributing to the development of drama.

keyAchievements.title: Political and Military Activities
keyAchievements.description: He participated in political life before and after the Peloponnesian War, serving as a commander of the Delian League and a naval admiral.

keyAchievements.title: Extant Works
keyAchievements.description: Seven tragic plays survive: 'Women of Trachis', 'Ajax', 'Antigone', 'Oedipus Rex', 'Electra', 'Philoctetes', and 'Oedipus at Colonus'.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Hero Title
keyAchievements.description: After his death, he received the hero title of 'Dexion' from the citizens of Athens.

faq.question: When was Sophocles born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Sophocles was born in 497 BC and died in 406 BC.

faq.question: How is Sophocles evaluated?
faq.answer: As a tragic poet of ancient Athens, Greece, he is considered one of the three great Greek tragic poets alongside Aeschylus and Euripides.

faq.question: What contributions did Sophocles make to tragic art?
faq.answer: Known as the perfecter of tragic art, he contributed to the development of drama by abolishing the trilogy format, increasing the chorus members, and introducing a third actor.

faq.question: What are the representative extant works of Sophocles?
faq.answer: There are 7 extant works: 'Women of Trachis', 'Ajax', 'Antigone', 'Oedipus Rex', 'Electra', 'Philoctetes', and 'Oedipus at Colonus'.

faq.question: Did Sophocles also engage in political activities?
faq.answer: He participated in political life before and after the Peloponnesian War, serving as a commander of the Delian League and a naval admiral.

timeline.event: Birth of Euclid
Active in Alexandria during the reign of Ptolemy I Soter
Death of Euclid

keyAchievements.title: 'Elements'
keyAchievements.description: Consisting of 13 books covering geometry and number theory, it is one of the most influential works in the history of mathematics due to its logical progression deriving results from a set of axioms.

keyAchievements.title: 'Data'
keyAchievements.description: A treatise dealing with geometry.

keyAchievements.title: 'Phaenomena'
keyAchievements.description: A treatise dealing with spherical geometry.

keyAchievements.title: 'Optics'
keyAchievements.description: The oldest surviving Greek treatise on optics.

keyAchievements.title: 'On Divisions of Figures'
keyAchievements.description: Deals with the problem of dividing a given figure in a given ratio; it survives only partially in an Arabic translation.

keyAchievements.title: 'Catoptrics'
keyAchievements.description: Deals with geometric problems using mirrors.

faq.question: Is there an anecdote about Euclid's disciple asking about the practicality of mathematics?
faq.answer: There is a famous anecdote that when a disciple asked about the practicality of mathematics during Euclid's lecture, Euclid gave him three coins and told him to be kicked out of the lecture hall.

timeline.event: Born as Mdungazwe, son of King Mzila of the Gaza Empire.
After his father's death, defeated his brothers in a war of succession, ascended the throne, and took the name Gungunhana.
Moved the capital from the north to Manjacaze in the south to be closer to the center of Portuguese influence.
A Portuguese army led by Joaquim Mouzinho de Albuquerque declared war, and Gungunhana was captured in the sacred village of Chaimite in December.
After being paraded in Lisbon, he was exiled to the Azores, a Portuguese archipelago in the Atlantic.
Died in exile on Terceira Island in the Azores.
His remains were repatriated to Mozambique and reburied with honors as a national hero.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of the Gaza Empire
keyAchievements.description: Following a succession crisis, successfully united the empire and established authority over rival factions.

keyAchievements.title: Skillful Diplomacy
keyAchievements.description: Adeptly attempted to play the colonial interests of Britain and Portugal against each other to preserve his empire's independence.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of Resistance
keyAchievements.description: Although ultimately unsuccessful, his resolute resistance to Portuguese colonization made him a national hero of Mozambique and a symbol of the anti-colonial struggle.

faq.question: Why is he called the 'Lion of Gaza'?
faq.answer: The name reflects his fierce reputation as a warrior and his resolute resistance against the Portuguese, symbolizing the strength and pride of his Gaza Empire.

faq.question: Did Gungunhana have any allies?
faq.answer: He attempted to form an alliance with the British South Africa Company led by Cecil Rhodes to counter the Portuguese. However, these diplomatic efforts ultimately failed to provide the military support he needed.

faq.question: What happened to his body after his death?
faq.answer: He was buried in the Azores. In 1985, his remains were repatriated to Mozambique and reburied with honors as a national hero, solidifying his legacy.

timeline.event: Born as the daughter of Usman dan Fodio, the founder of the Sokoto Caliphate.
Lived through the Fulani War which led to the establishment of the Sokoto Caliphate.
Established the 'jaji' (or 'Yan Taru) network of itinerant female teachers.
Her brother Muhammad Bello became the second Caliph, and she served as one of his most trusted advisors.
Died in Sokoto, leaving a legacy of over 60 written works and an innovative educational system.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Jaji ('Yan Taru) Network
keyAchievements.description: She built a system of itinerant female teachers to educate women in rural areas across the Caliphate.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Poet and Scholar
keyAchievements.description: She authored over 60 works in Arabic, Fula, and Hausa, recording history, law, and theology.

keyAchievements.title: Advisor to the Caliph
keyAchievements.description: She served as a trusted advisor to her brother, Caliph Muhammad Bello, influencing state policies on education and social welfare.

faq.question: What does 'Nana' in her name mean?
faq.answer: 'Nana' is an honorific title similar to princess or lady, and is not her real name. Her real name was Asma'u bint Usman dan Fodio.

faq.question: What languages did Nana Asma'u speak?
faq.answer: She was a polyglot, fluent in at least four languages: Arabic, Fula (Fulfulde), Hausa, and Tamasheq (Tuareg).

faq.question: Is her legacy still relevant today?
faq.answer: Yes, her work is a vital historical precedent for Muslim feminism and women's education in Africa, and her educational movement continues to inspire several modern initiatives.

timeline.event: Born in Egypt, though her exact origins remain unclear.
Married Pharaoh Amenhotep IV, who later changed his name to Akhenaten.
Akhenaten began his religious revolution. The royal court moved to the new capital Akhetaten (modern Amarna).
Nefertiti vanished from the surviving historical record. Her fate is unknown.
After Akhenaten's death, the old gods were gradually restored under the rule of Tutankhamun.
The famous painted limestone bust of Nefertiti was discovered in Amarna by German archaeologist Ludwig Borchardt.

keyAchievements.title: Religious Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Co-led a radical shift from traditional Egyptian polytheism to the monotheistic worship of the sun god Aten.

keyAchievements.title: Co-ruler of Egypt
keyAchievements.description: Wielded immense power and was often depicted in poses signifying pharaonic authority, suggesting near-equal status with her husband Akhenaten.

keyAchievements.title: Icon of Amarna Art
keyAchievements.description: Became the central subject of a new naturalistic art style that portrayed the royal family with unprecedented intimacy and realism.

faq.question: What does your name mean?
faq.answer: My name, Nefertiti, means 'The beautiful one has come' in ancient Egyptian.

faq.question: Why did you and Akhenaten worship only one god?
faq.answer: We believed that Aten, the sun disk, was the universal creator and sustainer of all life, not just in Egypt, but across the world. We sought to replace the complex, and in our view, corrupt old gods with this single, pure source of light and truth.

faq.question: What happened to you?
faq.answer: My fate after my husband's 12th regnal year is a mystery lost to the sands of time. Some believe I died, others think I fell out of favor, and some even speculate that I subsequently ruled as pharaoh. The records are silent, but my legacy endures forever.

timeline.event: Traditional date when Dido is said to have founded Carthage according to ancient records.
Traditional date of Dido's death according to the historian Timaeus.
The Roman poet Virgil wrote the epic 'Aeneid', which contains the most famous and influential account of Dido's life and her tragic love affair with Aeneas.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Carthage
keyAchievements.description: She led her people from Tyre and, through wisdom and leadership, established one of the most powerful and enduring cities of the ancient world.

keyAchievements.title: The Oxhide Negotiation
keyAchievements.description: Her legendary wit in cutting an oxhide into thin strips to enclose a large tract of land became a symbol of ingenuity and the foundation of her new kingdom.

keyAchievements.title: Archetype of the Tragic Heroine
keyAchievements.description: Her story in Virgil's 'Aeneid' immortalized her as the archetype of a powerful woman consumed by tragic love and betrayal, influencing Western art and literature for centuries.

faq.question: Was Dido a real person?
faq.answer: Her historical existence is debated. While some ancient Greek and Roman historians recorded her as a historical figure, the most famous account of her life in Virgil's 'Aeneid' is a literary creation blending myth and historical elements.

faq.question: Why did Dido take her own life?
faq.answer: In Virgil's 'Aeneid', she commits suicide out of heartbreak after Aeneas abandons her to fulfill his destiny in Italy. In older legends, it is said she took her own life to avoid a forced marriage to a local king and remain faithful to her dead husband.

faq.question: What is the significance of Dido's curse?
faq.answer: In the 'Aeneid', the curse Dido places upon Aeneas and his descendants serves as a mythological origin story for the bitter rivalry and the three Punic Wars fought between Carthage and Rome.

timeline.event: Born as Ramavo to non-royal parents.
Ascended the throne of the Kingdom of Madagascar after the death of her husband, King Radama I, taking the name Ranavalona.
Issued an edict banning the Christian faith in Madagascar, leading to the persecution of Malagasy Christians.
Successfully repelled a joint French-British naval attack on the port of Tamatave, demonstrating her military strength.
A coup attempt orchestrated by her son Radama II and European aides like Jean Laborde and Joseph-François Lambert was discovered and thwarted.
Died in her sleep, ending a 33-year reign. She was succeeded by her son Radama II, who reversed her isolationist policies.

keyAchievements.title: Preservation of Sovereignty
keyAchievements.description: Successfully resisted the encroachment of European colonialism for over three decades, preserving Madagascar's independence long after many other nations had fallen.

keyAchievements.title: Industrial Self-Sufficiency
keyAchievements.description: Established local industrial complexes producing modern weapons, textiles, and other goods, reducing reliance on foreign imports.

keyAchievements.title: Military Modernization
keyAchievements.description: Built and maintained a large, well-trained standing army armed with locally manufactured weapons, enabling the defense of the island from foreign invasion.

faq.question: Why was Ranavalona I called the 'Mad Queen' or 'Female Caligula' by Europeans?
faq.answer: Her policies, particularly the violent persecution of Christians and the widespread use of the 'tangena' poison ordeal which led to the deaths of thousands, were viewed by Europeans as barbaric and tyrannical. This narrative also served to delegitimize her rule and justify potential colonial intervention.

faq.question: What was the 'tangena' ordeal?
faq.answer: It was a traditional form of trial by ordeal where the accused was forced to consume a poison extracted from the nut of the tangena shrub. Survival was considered proof of innocence, and death meant guilt. Ranavalona used it extensively to eliminate political opponents and enforce social and religious conformity.

faq.question: Did she really hate all foreigners?
faq.answer: Not entirely. While she harbored deep suspicions of European governments and expelled missionaries, she selectively employed certain foreigners whom she deemed essential. The most notable example is the Frenchman Jean Laborde, who played a crucial role in developing her industrial and military complexes.

faq.question: What is her legacy in Madagascar?
faq.answer: Her legacy is complex and highly debated. Some view her as a cruel tyrant whose policies led to the deaths of a significant portion of the population. Others see her as a national heroine who fiercely defended Malagasy sovereignty and traditions against the tide of European colonialism, preserving the nation's independence for decades.

timeline.event: Born in Roha, Ethiopia. Legend says that at his birth, a swarm of bees surrounded him, prompting his mother to name him Lalibela ('the bees recognize his sovereignty').
Went into exile due to conflict with his older half-brother, King Harbay.
Survived a poisoning attempt, during which he is said to have seen a divine vision of rock-hewn churches.
Ruled as Emperor of Ethiopia after his brother abdicated the throne to him.
Commissioned and oversaw the construction of 11 monolithic rock-hewn churches in his capital, which was renamed Lalibela in his honor.
Died after abdicating the throne to spend his final years as a hermit. He was later canonized as a saint by the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church.

keyAchievements.title: Construction of Rock-Hewn Churches
keyAchievements.description: Commissioned the carving of 11 monolithic churches from solid rock, creating a UNESCO World Heritage site.

keyAchievements.title: Building a 'New Jerusalem'
keyAchievements.description: Established his capital as a major Christian pilgrimage site intended to replace Jerusalem.

keyAchievements.title: Canonization as a Saint
keyAchievements.description: Revered as one of the most important saints in the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church for his piety and monumental architectural achievements of faith.

faq.question: Are the churches of Lalibela really built from a single rock?
faq.answer: Yes, the churches are monolithic structures, meaning they were carved from a single, solid block of volcanic tuff rock, excavated downward into the earth rather than built upwards from the ground.

faq.question: Why is Lalibela called a 'New Jerusalem'?
faq.answer: When Saladin captured Jerusalem in 1187, making pilgrimage difficult and dangerous, Emperor Lalibela intended his city to be an alternative pilgrimage site for Christians. Its layout and even the names of its churches mimic those of Jerusalem.

faq.question: Is it true that angels helped build the churches?
faq.answer: According to Ethiopian tradition and hagiography, angels worked alongside human laborers every night, doubling the daily progress. This legend accounts for the seemingly impossible speed and scale of the construction.

timeline.event: Born as al-Hasan ibn Muhammad al-Wazzan in Granada, shortly before or after the fall of the city.
His family was expelled from Granada and settled in Fez, Morocco.
Accompanied his uncle on diplomatic missions across North Africa, reaching Timbuktu and the Songhai Empire.
Undertook another major journey, visiting Constantinople and traveling up the Nile in Egypt.
Captured by Christian pirates at sea and taken to Rome.
Baptized by Pope Leo X in St. Peter's Basilica, receiving the name Joannes Leo de Medicis.
Completed the manuscript of his major work, 'Description of Africa', in Italian.
Left Rome. His later years are uncertain, with some historians believing he returned to Tunis.
His 'Description of Africa' was first published in Venice by Giovanni Battista Ramusio.
Estimated year of death, though the exact date and location remain unknown.

keyAchievements.title: Description of Africa (Della descrittione dell'Africa)
keyAchievements.description: Authored the most comprehensive and influential account of North African geography, culture, and politics available to Europeans for centuries.

keyAchievements.title: Cultural Mediator
keyAchievements.description: Acted as a unique bridge between the Islamic world of North Africa and the Christian world of Renaissance Europe through his life and work.

keyAchievements.title: Polyglot Scholar
keyAchievements.description: His fluency in multiple languages, including Arabic, Italian, and likely Spanish and Hebrew, enabled his academic and diplomatic activities across diverse cultures.

faq.question: Was Leo Africanus his real name?
faq.answer: No. His original name was al-Hasan ibn Muhammad al-Wazzan. He received the name Joannes Leo de Medicis when baptized by Pope Leo X, and became known as Leo Africanus (Leo the African) after the publication of his famous book.

faq.question: Why was his book so important?
faq.answer: Prior to his work, European knowledge of the African interior was based on ancient texts and sparse rumors. His book provided detailed, primary source information on major cities like Timbuktu, trade routes, political structures, and local customs based on his firsthand travels and observations.

faq.question: Did he remain a Christian?
faq.answer: It is unknown. After leaving Rome around 1529, his whereabouts are unclear. Some historians believe he may have returned to North Africa (Tunis) and reconverted to Islam, but there is no conclusive evidence.
